title: NATO Officially Confirms Rutte Will Meet Trump, Rubio, and Hegseth on April 8
detail: NATO officially confirmed via media advisory that Secretary General Mark Rutte would visit Washington April 8–12. On April 8 he would hold face-to-face meetings with President Trump, Secretary of State Rubio, and Defense Secretary Hegseth — the full senior US security leadership. Rutte was also scheduled to address the Reagan Presidential Foundation on April 9 and participate in Bilderberg meetings April 10–12. The confirmation came six days after Trump declared NATO a 'paper tiger' and stated he was 'strongly considering' US withdrawal. Rutte's pre-trip framing was deliberately de-escalatory: he told reporters Trump was 'just frustrated' with allies. EU News headlined the visit as 'Rutte meets Trump in face-to-face talks amid US-NATO withdrawal threats.' Analysts noted the meeting was make-or-break for the NATO-US relationship in the near term.

media.caption: NATO SG Rutte to meet Trump, Rubio, Hegseth amid US withdrawal threat

title: Poland's Sikorski Opposes EU Nuclear Acquisition, Defends NATO Sharing Structure
detail: Polish Foreign Minister Radoslaw Sikorski publicly opposed EU countries acquiring independent nuclear weapons in response to the NATO crisis, calling it 'a risky idea' that would trigger proliferation by other states. Sikorski argued that NATO's existing nuclear-sharing system — hosting US B61 tactical nuclear bombs at bases in Belgium, Germany, Italy, Netherlands, and Turkey — already served deterrence effectively. The statement came in the context of French President Macron's March 2, 2026 announcement expanding France's arsenal and offering a 'forward deterrence' nuclear umbrella to European allies. Sikorski acknowledged that Britain and France's smaller arsenals differ significantly from the US nuclear stockpile, implicitly questioning whether a European nuclear guarantee could substitute for the American one. The Polish position highlighted tensions between France's autonomy push and eastern European states' preference for US-anchored deterrence.
